verdicts.note: Her only explicit philosophy statement is the mixed/middle framing the contest dossier itself describes: constitution, statutes, and precedent 'not personal views' (restraint language), qualified by openness to reform. The +1 leans on her ACLU/defense career to tilt an explicitly middle statement toward pole_b — career formation is sanctioned evidence for safety, not for interpretive philosophy, and her remaining platform (rights, access to justice, independence) is standard judicial boilerplate. 0 (genuinely mixed) is what the documented statement supports.

review_notes: Diaz judicial +2 rests on a single quoted phrase, but the rubric's pole_b description ('law meeting the community where it is') matches his stated philosophy nearly verbatim and medium confidence correctly reflects the single-statement basis. Diaz safety 0/low is genuinely mixed formation (10-year federal prosecutor vs. civil-rights-unit/police-reform focus), not a disguised unknown, and low confidence keeps it out of alignment math. Stevens safety -1 is carried by a documented bail-adjacent position (FTA warrants) plus law-enforcement support against a mixed prosecutor/defender formation; the race-based-jury-selection plank in the basis is philosophy evidence rather than safety evidence, but the score survives without it. No score in this file relies on the candidates' party recommendations.
